elements.sent-2411-001: Speculative execution is crucial in enhancing modern processor performance but can introduce Spectre-type vulnerabilities that may leak sensitive information.
elements.sent-2411-002: Detecting Spectre gadgets from programs has been a research focus to enhance the analysis and understanding of Spectre attacks.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, one of the problems of existing approaches is that they rely on the presence of source code (or are impractical in terms of run-time performance and gadget detection ability).
elements.sent-2411-004: This paper presents \toolname{ , the first Spectre gadget scanner that works on COTS binaries with comparable performance to compiler-based alternatives.}
elements.sent-2411-005: As its core principle, we introduce \twocopyname{ , a novel approach that separates the binary code for normal execution and speculation simulation in order to improve run-time efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-006: It instruments the program to simulate the effects of speculative execution and also adds integrity checks to detect Spectre gadgets at run time.
elements.sent-2411-007: By leveraging fuzzing, \toolname{ succeeds in efficiently detecting Spectre gadgets.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Evaluations show that \toolname{ outperforms both performance (more than 20$\times$ performant) and gadget detection ability than a previously proposed binary-based approach.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Transient execution attacks such as Spectre\cite{SpectrePHTBTB have exposed a new threat to system security.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Secrets can even be leaked from functionally correct programs by abusing speculative execution, which is one of the powerhouses driving today's microprocessor performance.
elements.sent-2411-011: Specifically, an attacker could carefully craft branch misprediction(s) to exert code snippets which are bug-free in normal execution, to speculatively run into unintended control flows and read secret information.
elements.sent-2411-012: Code snippets potentially vulnerable to such attacks are called Spectre gadgets.
elements.sent-2411-013: Identifying Spectre gadgets in programs has been a focus of many researchers\cite{SpecFuzz,SpecTaint,Kasper,oo7,Spectector,Cats .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Much effort has been put towards detecting them by assuming the source code is accessible.
elements.sent-2411-015: State-of-the-art dynamic Spectre gadget detectors that utilize the source code include SpecFuzz\cite{SpecFuzz and Kasper\cite{Kasper ; they rely on the compiler to statically instrument the program in order to simulate the consequences of speculative execution (we call it speculation simulation for short) and dynamically detect gadgets at run time.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: They expand detection coverage with the help of fuzzers.
elements.sent-2411-017: Such tools are helpful when the source code is present, but it is not always the case.\looseness=-1 Tools that find gadgets from binaries become paramount when analyzing closed-source security-critical applications, such as banking and cryptocurrency apps, password managers, and device drivers.
elements.sent-2411-018: They allow researchers to improve the analysis and their understanding of Spectre gadgets in the wild.
elements.sent-2411-019: Not only when dealing with closed-source programs is binary analysis valuable: it allows inspecting the actual deployed application; compilers and toolchains are not necessarily bug-free, and thus properties proven by static source code analysis might not hold for the compiled version\cite{angr .\looseness=-1 Unfortunately, the only work that operates at the binary level as of this writing is SpecTaint\cite{SpecTaint , which lacks both run-time efficiency and program-level information, since it is based on a full-system emulator.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: In order to advance research in this area, we set our goal to develop a practical binary-based dynamic Spectre gadget detector that works on commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) binaries without the presence of source code.
elements.sent-2411-021: To this end, we present \toolname{ , which is based on static binary rewriting, designed with efficiency as its guiding principle.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Teapot's efficiency can be attributed to a key insight derived from the following observation.
elements.sent-2411-023: Existing approaches have a common problem: normal execution and speculation simulation that require totally different instrumentations coexist inside a single instance of the final instrumented code, and thus every single instrumentation is guarded with an if condition.
elements.sent-2411-024: This adds significant performance overhead since the program needs to execute a huge number of branch instructions at run time.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our key insight is that we can eliminate almost all the guard conditions and greatly accelerate run-time performance by separating the binary code executed in normal execution and speculation simulation.
elements.sent-2411-027: We present the detailed design and evaluate the performance of \toolname{ .}
elements.sent-2411-028: It significantly outperforms SpecTaint in both execution efficiency and gadget detection ability; in addition, despite being a binary-only approach, \toolname{ yields results comparable to compiler-based instrumentations, proving its practical viability.}
elements.sent-2411-030: We propose \twocopyname{ , the framework and design principle behind \toolname{ .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: It is engineered to support static binary instrumentation for detecting Spectre gadgets, by completely separating normal execution and speculation simulation code.
elements.sent-2411-032: We utilize \twocopyname{ to implement and open source \toolname{ , the first Spectre-V1 gadget detector based on static binary rewriting, designed to be highly efficient.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: We evaluate \toolname{ and show that it outperforms the state-of-the-art binary-based approach SpecTaint in run-time efficiency (more than 20$\times$ performant) and detection ability of Spectre gadgets in COTS binaries.}
elements.lstlisting-2411-001: [style=my-lst-style-linenum, caption=A potential Spectre-V1 gadget., float=tp, label={lst:gadget}, belowskip=-1 \baselineskip] int index = user_input(); if (index < 10) { // B1: Mispredicted secret = foo[index]; // L1: Load Secret baz = bar[secret]; // L2: Transmit Secret }
elements.sent-2411-034: Modern out-of-order microprocessors rely on many forms of speculation when executing programs to enhance performance.
elements.sent-2411-035: For instance, a processor may encounter a branch instruction whose direction depends on preceding instructions yet to be completed.
elements.sent-2411-036: In such case, it consults the branch predictor to predict the correct direction (and address) and speculatively continues execution along the predicted path.
elements.sent-2411-037: If the prediction turns out to be correct, the processor gains performance over waiting for the branch direction to be resolved.
elements.sent-2411-038: Otherwise, it discards all the results that are executed speculatively (or transiently) as if they have not been executed, and resumes along the correct path.
elements.sent-2411-039: Spectre attacks leverage such transient executions to leak secret information from programs.
elements.sent-2411-040: The vulnerability lies in the implementation of microprocessors when discarding the results of transiently executed instructions.
elements.sent-2411-041: Although they successfully remove architectural changes, traces might be left in the microarchitecture resources such as the caches (which might be later inferred via timing covert/side channels).
elements.sent-2411-042: The attacker uses this vulnerability to steal sensitive data from a victim program by letting the processor transiently access the secret.
elements.sent-2411-043: We use the canonical Spectre-V1 (aka bounds check bypass) gadget\cite{SpectrePHTBTB shown in \cref{lst:gadget to demonstrate how the attack could hypothetically work.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: First, the attacker feeds index with values smaller than 10 to train the branch predictor to make a prediction that the if condition (bounds check; B1) holds.
elements.sent-2411-045: Next, it supplies a malicious index value greater than 10 to induce a branch misprediction that transiently allows bypassing the bounds check to access a secret value into a register (L1).
elements.sent-2411-046: The secret value is further used as an index of the next memory access, encoding the value as the location inside the cache (as a cache set ID; L2).
elements.sent-2411-047: Even after the transiently executed instructions are architecturally wiped out, the attacker can learn the secret value by performing cache attacks such as the Prime+Probe attack\cite{EVICT+TIME_PRIME+PROBE .}

elements.sent-2411-048: While all conditional branches are potential victims of \mbox{Spectre-V1 , they can be exploited to leak secret information only if the incorrect execution path meets certain criteria (such as the one shown in \cref{lst:gadget ).}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Finding such gadgets in real-world programs is not trivial as the criteria are complex.
elements.sent-2411-050: Static gadget scanning works that rely on matching program source with common Spectre code patterns\cite{oo7,MSVC can be ineffective, as they introduce false positives and false negatives due to complex variations of Spectre gadgets beyond the description of fixed patterns.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Formal methods and symbolic execution\cite{Spectector,SpecuSym,KLEESpectre,Cats ensure high detection coverage, but do not scale well with large real-world programs because of the large search space and high computational complexity\cite{SpecFuzz .}}
elements.sent-2411-052: They instrument the program to simulate the effects of branch misprediction, add integrity checks to flag Spectre gadgets, and provide random inputs to test the program (with fuzzing).
elements.sent-2411-053: At a high level they share a similar software architecture shown in \cref{lst:specfuzz .}
elements.sent-2411-054: It stores the current program state before each conditional branch.
elements.sent-2411-055: To simulate branch misprediction, it forces the program onto the reverted branch direction.
elements.sent-2411-056: Then, speculative execution is simulated by letting the program proceed on the wrong path for some instructions until a rollback point is reached.
elements.sent-2411-057: The program state from the checkpoint is restored, and normal execution resumes.
elements.sent-2411-058: We introduce the three major works in this category in the following:.
elements.sent-2411-059: SpecFuzz\cite{SpecFuzz is the first to propose dynamically simulating the effects of branch misprediction in software to detect Spectre gadgets.}
elements.sent-2411-060: It does so by adding instrumentation to the analyzed programs during compilation (and thus requires source code).
elements.sent-2411-061: During speculation simulation, SpecFuzz detects out-of-bounds memory errors with AddressSanitizer~(ASan)\cite{Asan and flags all of them as gadgets.}
elements.sent-2411-062: SpecTaint\cite{SpecTaint is currently the only dynamic binary analysis tool for detecting Spectre gadgets and hence our direct competitor.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Its program instrumentation mechanisms are built on top of DECAF\cite{DECAF , an extension of the full-system emulator QEMU\cite{QEMU .}}
elements.sent-2411-064: SpecTaint introduces dynamic information flow tracking~(DIFT)\cite{DIFT to mark user inputs and trace the information flow to detect gadgets.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Kasper\cite{Kasper takes a principled approach and currently achieves the highest coverage in terms of gadget detection ability.}
elements.sent-2411-066: It is specifically built for testing the Linux kernel and adds instrumentation during compilation (and thus requires source code).
elements.sent-2411-067: To detect attacker controllability and identify secrets, Kasper tracks the data flow by employing DIFT and implements kernel support for the DataFlowSanitizer\cite{DFSan of LLVM.\ It also utilizes ASan to identify out-of-bounds accesses.}
elements.sent-2411-068: It considers both user-controlled input and speculatively uncontrolled out-of-bounds access to potentially load a secret.
elements.sent-2411-069: In addition to the cache side channel, it also includes microarchitectural data sampling~(MDS)\cite{RIDL,Fallout and port contention channels.}
elements.sent-2411-070: We adopt the Kasper policy to \toolname{ in this work for its prominent detection coverage and accuracy, further described in \cref{ssec:nahco3-gadget .. \looseness=-1.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: We assume the source code of the target program is missing.
elements.sent-2411-072: Except for this assumption, we share a similar threat model with various other Spectre gadget detection studies\cite{SpecFuzz,SpecTaint,Kasper .}
elements.sent-2411-073: We assume that the application is a potential victim of speculative information leaks.
elements.sent-2411-074: To simplify the problem setting, we aim to detect Spectre-V1 gadgets leaking through the cache, MDS, and port contention side channels.
elements.sent-2411-075: Although the proposed system can be extended to support other variants, most already have production-grade mitigations\cite{IntelSpectreMitigations ; in contrast, Spectre-V1 still cannot be easily mitigated with relatively low overhead.}
elements.sent-2411-076: We do not attempt to detect non-Spectre type vulnerabilities (e.g.,~memory safety violations) and assume they do not exist in the target application.
elements.sent-2411-077: Following a previous study\cite{SpecFuzz , we also do not attempt to detect Spectre gadgets guarded by more than six branches (including the first branch misprediction) as such gadgets are unlikely to be exploitable in the real world.}

elements.sent-2411-078: As discussed earlier, the only available dynamic binary analysis tool for detecting Spectre gadgets is SpecTaint\cite{SpecTaint .}
elements.sent-2411-079: Speaking of practicality, the problem with SpecTaint lies in its QEMU-based implementation; it inevitably limits its run-time efficiency, ultimately hindering gadget detection ability.
elements.sent-2411-080: We demonstrate this by comparing the run-time performance of SpecTaint vs.\ SpecFuzz (a compiler-based approach).
elements.sent-2411-081: We instrument two test programs, namely libyaml and jsmn and evaluate their execution times with crafted large inputs.
elements.sent-2411-082: For a fair comparison, we disable their nested speculation simulation (described further in \cref{ssec:nahco3-ckpt-support ) and heuristic simulation skipping mechanisms.}
elements.sent-2411-084: While SpecFuzz, an LLVM-based analysis tool, inevitably shows a large performance overhead (${\approx 600\times$ and ${\approx 1,800\times$) mainly due to speculation simulation, SpecTaint is another order of magnitude slower: compared to SpecFuzz, it is 28.5$\times$ and 11.1$\times$ slower on libyaml and jsmn, respectively.\looseness=-1 Due to its large performance overhead, SpecTaint trades off detection ability and only simulates the misprediction of each branch up to five times.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: This leaves many potential false negatives on the table (details in \cref{ssec:exp-fuzz-v2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-086: In addition, being based on a system emulator, SpecTaint lacks knowledge of critical program data structures such as the heap and stack.
elements.sent-2411-087: It also cannot differentiate out-of-bounds and legal access.
elements.sent-2411-088: Therefore, it has to be overly restrictive and assume all user-controlled memory accesses load secrets, resulting in many false positives, as shown in a previous study\cite{Kasper .\looseness=-1.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Since both SpecFuzz and Kasper leverage LLVM compiler passes for instrumenting programs, they do not pertain to our threat model.
elements.sent-2411-090: However, for the sake of demonstrating \toolname{ 's effectiveness, we perform discussions and comparisons against SpecFuzz in terms of performance and gadget detection ability throughout this paper (Kasper cannot be compared since it is tailored for analyzing Linux kernel).}
elements.sent-2411-091: Generally speaking, using source code and information from the compiler for instrumentation can be more effective than static binary rewriting.
elements.sent-2411-092: However, our observations highlight overlooked aspects by previous works, bringing attention that compiler-based methods have their own limitations and require careful application.
elements.sent-2411-093: First, we illustrate one example by compiling a switch statement with GCC and Clang.
elements.sent-2411-094: The respective assembly instructions are shown in \cref{fig:switch-compiled-example .}
elements.sent-2411-095: The statement may be compiled into a combination of branches or a jump table depending on the compiler; only the former may result in Spectre-V1 gadgets.
elements.sent-2411-096: If the analysis is done with Clang (as in SpecFuzz or Kasper), the generated program will not contain any gadgets.
elements.sent-2411-097: However, if the final deployed programs are compiled with GCC instead, some gadgets might go undetected.
elements.sent-2411-098: This means that compiler instrumentation-based approaches can be prone to false negatives and false positives due to the mismatch of compilers (and/or optimization levels) used for analysis vs.\ deployment.
elements.sent-2411-099: We make another point that compiler-based methods can be inaccurate due to instrumentations commonly taking place before optimizations.
elements.sent-2411-100: As a concrete example, SpecFuzz relies on ASan (to detect out-of-bounds accesses), whose instrumentations happen at the compiler frontend.
elements.sent-2411-101: It also implements most of its instrumentation passes in the compiler backend.
elements.sent-2411-102: Since its speculation simulation instrumentations happen in the backend, it cannot distinguish the original program code vs.\ ASan instrumented code.
elements.sent-2411-103: This hurts run-time performance (and hence gadget detection ability) since meaningless instrumentations are added to ASan code; to make matters worse, ASan code is counted as program instructions, rendering the length of transient execution simulation (until the reorder buffer fills) inaccurate.
elements.sent-2411-104: Porting everything into the compiler backend will potentially solve the problem, but requires huge engineering effort.\looseness=-1.
elements.sent-2411-105: It accepts a user-space COTS binary program as an input and works without the presence of source code.
elements.sent-2411-106: In the first static rewriting stage, \toolname{ fits the binaries with static instrumentation that helps detect Spectre gadgets.}
elements.sent-2411-107: The second stage relies on existing techniques to perform dynamic fuzzing where the binaries are fuzzed with the instrumentation that dynamically detects gadgets at run time.
elements.sent-2411-108: In the following sections, we first present \twocopyname{ , our fundamental design concept that splits the program into Real Copy and Shadow Copy for enhancing performance in \cref{sec:twocopy .}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Next, we introduce the architecture and implementation of \toolname{ in \cref{sec:architecture .}}

elements.sent-2411-110: The problem is that different types of instrumentations for the two modes coexist in the program to detect Specter gadgets: instrumentation for creating checkpoints and flipping conditional branches in the normal execution mode; ASan checks, memory logging and rollback points in the speculation simulation mode.
elements.sent-2411-111: All the instrumentation codes are added to the same binary, guarded by conditional branches checking if the current mode matches what the instrumentation is intended for.\looseness=-1 Obviously, this form of instrumentation is not ideal because the guard condition if (in\_simulation) must be checked for each instrumentation at run time.
elements.sent-2411-112: Roughly speaking around 25\% of dynamically executed instructions are loads (require ASan check) and 10\% are stores (require ASan check and memory logging) in integer programs\cite{HennessyPatterson .}
elements.sent-2411-113: In addition, there are other forms of instrumentation added to the program (explained later).
elements.sent-2411-114: The above factors combined significantly slow down the program execution (and worsen fuzzing efficiency).
elements.sent-2411-115: The key insight of \twocopyname{ is that, by completely separating the binary code executed during normal execution and speculation simulation, we can equip them with only the needed instrumentation types.}
elements.sent-2411-116: This eliminates the need for conditional guards around each instrumentation, making the instrumented binary efficient and performant.. \looseness=-1.

elements.sent-2411-343: The Massage-Port gadget in \cref{lst:case-study-2 requires three nested misspeculations to exploit.}
elements.sent-2411-344: The exploitation starts with the normal execution on line 3, where list\_size is called.
elements.sent-2411-345: It is previously ensured that both conn and conn->txs are non-null.
elements.sent-2411-346: The function list\_size returns the size of the list, or the error code -1 when the list is a null pointer; while the list itself is non-null, if an attacker manages to induce a misspeculation on line 16, the function returns -1.
elements.sent-2411-347: The error code is then assigned to n (line 3), a size\_t variable.
elements.sent-2411-348: The assignment to -1 unexpectedly makes n the maximum value of size\_t, virtually making the loop never terminating, and enabling the attacker to speculatively read data out of bounds.
elements.sent-2411-349: Note that this assignment never happens in normal execution as conn->txs is ensured to be non-null.
elements.sent-2411-350: Then, line 5 fetches the i-th element from the list by calling list\_get.
elements.sent-2411-351: To read data out-of-bounds, the bounds checks at lines 23 and 24 must also be bypassed, requiring another two misspeculations.
elements.sent-2411-352: This results in tx being a massaged pointer constructed from an out-of-bounds read, which is then passed into the htp\_conn{\textunderscore\allowbreakremove\_tx function.}
elements.sent-2411-353: On line 31, some secret data is loaded into tx2 through a series of pointer dereferencing and array accesses, which is followed by a conditional branch on line 32, completing the leak of the secret data through port contention.
elements.sent-2411-355: Detection of this gadget with past Spectre detector implementations is extremely challenging if not impossible.
